He ranas fastas his shortlegs could carry him. The kite went up inthe air. It fell to theground witha bump. Toad heard laughter. Three robins were sitting ina bush "Thatkitewillnotfly," saidtherobins. ''Youmayaswellgive up." Toad ran backto Frog, "Frog," said Toad, "this kite willnot fly. I giveup. 9*) 19 ''We must makea second try," said Frog. "Wavethe kite over your head Perhaps that will make itfly." Toadran backacross the meadow, He wavedthekiteoverhis head. 20 Thekitewent up intheair andthenfell downwith athud "What a joke!"said therobins. "Thatkite willnever get offtheground." Toadranback to Frog. C4 ii CC This kiteis a joke,"he said. It will never get off theground." Wehave tomake a thirdtry," saidFrog. a Wavethe kiteoveryour head andjump up and down. Perhapsthat willmakeitfly." 22 Toad ran across themeadowagain Hewaved thekite overhishead. He jumpedup anddown. Thekite went up intheair andcrashed down intothe grass "That kiteis junk," said therobins. 'Throwit awayandgo home." Toad ran back to Frog. "This kiteisjunk," hesaid. 'T think weshould throw itaway andgo home." "Toad," said Frog, "weneed one moretry. Wave the kiteover your head. Jumpup anddown andshoutUP KITEUP." Toad ranacross the meadow. He wavedthe kiteover hishead Hejumpedup anddown. He shouted,"UP KITEUP!" 24 Thekite flewinto theair. It dimbed higher andhigher. 25 "We did it!" cried Toad. "Yes," said Frog. "If a running try did not work, anda running and wavingtry didnot work, anda running,waving, andjumpingtry didnotwork, I knewthat a running,waving, jumping, andshoutingtry just had to work." 26 The robinsflew out ofthe bush. ^ \ But they could notfly as high as thekite. FrogandToadsat and watchedtheirkite. It seemed to be flying way up at the top ofthesky. f^ / Shivers The nightwascold anddark. ''Listento the wind howUng inthetrees," saidFrog. "Whata finetime fora ghoststory." Toadmoved deeper into his chair. "Toad," asked Frog, "don't you hke to be scared? Don'tyou Uke to feelthe shivers?" "I amnot too sure," said Toad. 28 Frog made a fresh pot of tea, He sat down and began a story. 29 "When I was small,"said Frog, "my mother and father andI wentout fora picnic. On the way home welost our way. My mother was worried. 'Wemust get home,' shesaid. 'Wedo not want to meet the Old DarkFrog.' 'Whoisthat?'I asked. 30 'Aterribleghost,' said myfather. 'Hecomesoutat night andeats littlefrog childrenfor supper.' " Toadsipped his tea. 'Trog," he asked, "are you makingthis up?" "Maybeyes andmaybe no," saidFrog. 31 "My mother and father went to search fora path," said Frog. "They told meto wait until they came back. I sat under a tree and waited. The woods became dark. I was afraid. ThenI saw two huge eyes. It was the Old Dark Frog. 32 He was standing near me." 33 "Frog," asked Toad, ''did this really happen?" "Maybe itdid and maybe it didn't," said Frog. Frog wenton with thestory. "The Dark Frog pulled a jumprope outofhis pocket. 34 'I am not hungry now,' said the Dark Frog. 'I have eaten too many tasty frog children. But afterI jump rope one hundred times, I willbe hungry again. Then I will eat YOU!"' ii. The Dark Frog tied oneend of the rope to atree. Turn for me!'he shouted. I turned the ropefor the DarkFrog, He jumped twenty times. 'I am beginning to get hungry,' said theDark Frog. 36 He jumped fifty times. 'I am getting hungrier,' said the Dark Frog. He jumped ninety times. 'I am very hungry now!' said the Dark Frog. 95 ''What happened then?" asked Toad. 'Thad tosavemy hfe," said Frog. 44 I ran around and around the tree with the rope. Itied up the OldDark Frog. He roaredand screamed, 38 'JS^ r ^ff»ii^ I ran away fast. 99 39 "I found my mother and father," said Frog. ''We came safelyhome." y^'-d^i.'^ "Frog," askedToad, "wasthatatruestory?" "Maybeitwas and maybe itwasn't," saidFrog. 40 Fro2 and Toad sat close by the fire. They were scared. The teacups shook in their hands. They were having the shivers It was a good, warm feehng. Th£ Hat On Toad's birthday Frog gavehimahat. Toad was deUghted. ''Happy birthday," saidFrog. Toad put onthehat. Itfelldownover his eyes. 'T am sorry,"saidFrog. 'Thathatismuchtoo bigforyou, I will give you somethingelse." 42 r7^ W, WW (.(. No," said Toad. "This hat is your present to me. I Uke it. I will wear it the way it is." 43 Frog and Toad went fora walk, Toad tripped overa rock. He bumped intoa tree. He fell ina hole. 'Trog," saidToad, 'T can'tsee anything. I willnotbe ableto wear yourbeautiful present. Thisisasad birthday forme." 44 Frogand Toad were sad for a while. ThenFrogsaid. "Toad, here iswhat you mustdo, Tonightwhen yougo tobed you mustthink some verybig thoughts. Thosebig thoughts willmake yourhead grow larger. In the morning yournew hatmay fit." ''Whata good idea," saidToad. 45 Thatnight whenToad went tobed he thought thebiggest thoughts that he could think. Toad thought about giant sunflowers. He thought about talloaktrees. He thoughtabout highmountains covered withsnow. Then Toad fell asleep. Frog came into Toad's house He came inquietly. Frogfound thehat and took it to his house, 47 %^S/^ Frog poured some water onthe hat. He put thehat ina warm placeto dry. It began to shrink. That hat grew smallerand smaller. Frog went back toToad's house. Toadwasstill fastasleep. Frog putthehat backonthehook where hefoundit. When Toad woke up inthemorning, heputthehaton his head. It was just the right size 49 Toad ran to Frog's house. "Frog,Frog!" hecried. ''AH those bigthoughts havemademy head much larger. Now I canwearyour present!" FrogandToad wentfor a walk Toaddidnot trip overa rock. He did notbump intoa tree. He didnot fall in a hole. 50 It turned out to be a very pleasant day after Toad's birthday. 51 Alone ToadwenttoFrog's house. Hefoundanoteonthedoor. Thenote said, "Dear Toad,Iamnot at home. Iwent out.
